topic: The Impossible Collision That Created the Moon
title: How a Giant Impact May Have Built the Moon

lesson.learn:
The leading explanation for the Moon’s origin is the giant impact hypothesis. In that story, a Mars-sized body struck the young Earth, blasted material into orbit, and left behind a disk of debris that later merged into the Moon. The idea solves several puzzles at once, including the Moon’s composition and the Earth-Moon system’s angular momentum.

It sounds unbelievable because it is dramatic, but planet formation was a violent business. Big worlds were assembled through collisions, and the Moon may be the best surviving record of that chaos.
lesson.simpler: The Moon may have formed when a huge object hit early Earth. The debris from that impact slowly clumped together.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The Moon likely formed from debris created by a giant collision with early Earth.
lesson.deeperDive:
The giant impact hypothesis explains why the Moon and Earth share some isotopic similarities while also differing in important details. After the collision, hot debris orbited the planet, cooled, and accreted into a satellite. Over time, tidal interactions shaped the Earth-Moon system we know today.

The Moon’s origin matters because it helps us understand how rocky planets grow, how impacts redistribute material, and why Earth’s history cannot be separated from the violence of the early solar system.
lesson.funFact: The Moon is not a captured wandering rock.

people: William K. Hartmann - early giant impact proposer
Donald R. Davis - giant impact modeler
places: Early Earth orbit - debris disk formation
Lunar samples - evidence for formation models
Planetary science labs - impact simulation work
concepts: Giant impact hypothesis - Moon from collision debris
Accretion - matter clumping into a body
Angular momentum - rotational motion of a system
Isotopes - clues hidden in atomic variants
Tidal evolution - gravity slowly reshaping orbits
